original_abstract:
This book provides an extensive comparative analysis of "weak" presidents in Western Europe, uncovering the mechanisms through which these heads of state exercise influence despite limited constitutional authority. Drawing on a robust dataset from Austria, Germany, Finland, Ireland, Italy, and Portugal, Selena Grimaldi challenges the traditional dichotomy between ceremonial and executive presidencies by focusing on the emergence and efficacy of informal powers.

The study begins by mapping the profiles, career trajectories, and electoral legitimacy of these presidents, exploring how their diverse political backgrounds shape their institutional behavior. Central to the book is a new typology of informal powers—defined as substitutive, subverting, parallel, or risk-taking—that allows for a nuanced assessment of presidential activism in both government formation and the legislative process. Through detailed case studies and a systematic examination of presidential "going public" strategies, including a content analysis of major speeches during external shocks like the COVID-19 pandemic and the global financial crisis, the author reveals how presidents utilize moral suasion and "pocket vetoes" to navigate systemic crises. 

Ultimately, the research identifies the specific contextual factors and personal attributes that trigger successful presidential interventions. By bridging the gap between formal constitutional theory and actual political practice, this work offers a comprehensive framework for understanding the evolving role of the presidency in modern parliamentary and semi-presidential democracies.
humanized_abstract:
The Presidents’ Courts Western European leaders confront judicial power This book offers the first comprehensive comparative study of weak presidents in Western Europe and explains how these leaders exercise presidential power in the absence of constitutional formal authority. Using an innovative dataset consisting of 46 administrations in six countries (Austria, Germany, Finland, Ireland, Italy, and Portugal) between 1990 and 2015, Selena Grimaldi tests the presidency for courts against conventional expectations of ceremonial versus presidential roles, and explains how informal sources of power come to bear.

The Latin American Presidents: Regulation, Power and Legitimacy presents an in-depth study of the Latin American presidencies in the context of the fragmented multiparty systems. The book offers an in-depth examination of the profiles, presidential careers and electoral success of the presidents who have ruled these systems. It uses presidential action to analyze the institutional behavior of these presidents based on the political careers that presidents have had in previous political roles and their legitimacy at the polls. The study provides a new informal powers typology of Presidential action: substitutive, subverting, parallel and risk-taking. The exercise is complemented with particular case studies and the systematic analysis of the Presidential going public behaviour in relation to crucial policy decisions that correspond to extraordinary situations such as epidemics or economic crises. Such presidential communication and decision-making is the focus of a content analysis of some of the most important presidential speeches in relation to extraordinary events such as COVID-19 and the global financial crisis.

President’s Role in Government Crisis by examining in-depth the determinants of presidential intervention in political conflicts and crises This research aims ultimately to determine which context, presidential and prime-ministerial characteristics cause the president to have a successful intervention in the government crisis; thereby, bridging the gap between constitutional theories and political practice, and to contribute in depth to a more refined understanding of the role of the contemporary president in parliamentary and semi-presidential systems of government.
